result:
# The Weight of Unfinished Books

There's a particular kind of guilt that lives on nightstands. It accumulates in layers — one paperback on top of another, spines uncracked or cracked only at page forty-seven, bookmarks frozen in place like fossils marking the exact moment life interrupted.

I think about the optimism embedded in starting a book. You pick it up believing you're the kind of person who finishes things, who follows a stranger's thoughts all the way to their conclusion. You believe in future-you: that quiet evening version who doesn't scroll, doesn't get pulled away, who simply *reads*.

But that person rarely shows up.

What fascinates me isn't the guilt itself — it's that we keep buying them. We keep starting. Every unfinished book is evidence of someone who thought *this time will be different*, and I find that stubbornly beautiful. It's not failure. It's repeated faith in a version of yourself that you haven't met yet but keep expecting at the door.

Some of those abandoned books deserved to be abandoned. Not every writer earns your hours. But others — the ones you *liked*, the ones where you can still remember the last sentence you read — those haunt differently. They're not rejections. They're open tabs in your mind, quietly consuming background energy, whispering that the story is still waiting.

Maybe that's the real magic of books. They're patient in a way almost nothing else is. They don't unsubscribe you. They don't expire.

They just wait.

# The Weight of Unfinished Books

There's a particular kind of guilt that lives on nightstands. It accumulates in layers — one paperback on top of another, spines uncracked or cracked only at page forty-seven, bookmarks frozen in place like fossils marking the exact moment life interrupted.

I think about the optimism embedded in starting a book. You pick it up believing you're the kind of person who finishes things, who follows a stranger's thoughts all the way to their conclusion. You believe in future-you: that quiet evening version who doesn't scroll, doesn't get pulled away, who simply *reads*.

But that person rarely shows up.

What fascinates me isn't the guilt itself — it's that we keep buying them. We keep starting. Every unfinished book is evidence of someone who thought *this time will be different*, and I find that stubbornly beautiful. It's not failure. It's repeated faith in a version of yourself that you haven't met yet but keep expecting at the door.

Some of those abandoned books deserved to be abandoned. Not every writer earns your hours. But others — the ones you *liked*, the ones where you can still remember the last sentence you read — those haunt differently. They're not rejections. They're open tabs in your mind, quietly consuming background energy, whispering that the story is still waiting.

Maybe that's the real magic of books. They're patient in a way almost nothing else is. They don't unsubscribe you. They don't expire.

They just wait.
